text: ACT III
THE TRUST NETWORK
Intellectual and Social Sovereignty as the Foundation of a Just Economy
« An idea becomes property only when it materializes in the world. Before that, it belongs to the universe. After that, it should belong to the one who made it real. » — David Berthelotte
Introduction: From domestic sovereignty to intellectual sovereignty

Act II left us in the family living room — a space where artificial intelligence serves the inhabitants rather than surveilling them, where data stays within the walls that saw it born, where children grow up with the reflex of sovereignty as a natural muscle. But a household, even the most sovereign one, does not live in autarky. It creates. It produces. It invents. Its members work, compose, design, experiment. And every time a creation leaves the household to enter the world — every time an invention is shared, a composition is published, an innovation is put into practice — the question of sovereignty arises again, with renewed urgency. Who owns what I have created? How do I protect myself without blocking the spread of something that could serve the world?

This act explores the answer that UBLinx proposes — not as an airtight technical solution, but as a philosophy embodied in an architecture. We will meet Amadou, an inventor in Thiès, whom the global patent system left defenseless. We will follow Mei-Lin, a teenage musician in Montreal, who discovers that her creations can truly belong to her — not just in the romantic sense, but in the legal and economic sense. We will examine the mechanics of the «Fair Open Patent» — this third way between giving everything away and locking everything down. We will see how sovereign households connect into a network without submitting to a center. And we will pose, without a definitive answer, the question that runs through every system of protection: when the sovereignty of some also protects actors whose actions we would rather not protect, what do we do?

Before we enter the stories, a clarification is needed about the nature of UBLinx at this stage. It is not a commercial product available on the App Store. It is a conceptual architecture — a blueprint, a vision, a set of principles that have internal coherence and technical feasibility, but that have not yet gone through the fire of large-scale implementation. I am a Quebec inventor and technician with more than two hundred innovations documented in my notebooks, awaiting filing — not a prophet announcing certainties. What I am proposing is the direction. The road itself remains to be built.
Chapter 1
The inventor in his garage
« The patent was invented to liberate inventors. It has been turned against them as a weapon in the hands of those who no longer invent anything. »

Thiès, Senegal. June 2031. In a workshop measuring five meters by six, Amadou Diallo, thirty-four years old, carefully lifts a water filter he designed himself over the past eighteen months. The frame is made from nozzles salvaged from an abandoned construction site. The filter medium is composed of calcined sand, plant-based charcoal, and a membrane he wove from locally sourced fibers chemically treated using a method he spent four months refining. The test results analyzed at the University of Dakar are there, on his wooden table: a 99.3% reduction in fecal bacteria, 98.7% in parasites, and 94% in microplastics. This filter works. It truly works, with local materials, at a manufacturing cost of roughly 4,500 CFA francs — less than eight US dollars. It could save lives in the forty-seven villages of the department that lack access to clean drinking water.

Amadou sets the filter down gently. He opens his laptop, its screen bearing the marks of an old fall that was never repaired. He searches «how to protect an invention in Senegal.» The results inform him that filing a national patent costs between 500,000 and 800,000 CFA francs — the equivalent of eight to thirteen months of his average income. An international patent through the Patent Cooperation Treaty would cost ten times more. Processing times: two to five years. The language of the forms: the most hermetic legal French. Intellectual property law firms in Dakar: two, one of which demands a retainer equal to six months of Amadou’s income simply for an initial consultation. Amadou closes his laptop. The idea that could change lives — his own, those of thousands of people — is legally defenseless. He will go to bed tonight knowing that if a multinational water treatment company discovers his invention, it can industrialize it, patent it by claiming slight modifications, and leave him without practical recourse or compensation.

Amadou’s story is not an exception. It is the rule. In the informal laboratories of Lagos, in the workshops of Manila, in the garages of Trois-Rivières and Rimouski, thousands of individual inventors face the same brutal equation every year: their ideas are potentially worth millions, but the cost of protecting them far exceeds their means. The global patent system, designed in the 19th century to encourage innovation by guaranteeing inventors temporary exclusivity over their discoveries, has gradually transformed into something none of its architects would have wanted: an instrument of domination serving large corporations against individuals who dare to invent. WIPO (the World Intellectual Property Organization) itself acknowledges that fewer than 5% of inventors in developing countries gain effective protection for their innovations. This means that 95% of innovative ideas from the Global South are, in practice, freely given to the world — without any compensation for their authors.
The paradox of protection: a two-century history

The Paris Convention of 1883, which laid the foundations of the international patent system, started from a sound intuition: if an inventor knows that his invention will be guaranteed for twenty years, he will have no reason to keep it secret. He will publish it. Society will benefit from the knowledge, the inventor will benefit from the temporary monopoly. A fair exchange. A social contract between individual genius and the common good. For several decades, this logic partially worked. Independent inventors like Edison — who nevertheless had considerable resources and an entire laboratory of collaborators — filed patents that truly protected their innovations against copiers. The system was imperfect, but it roughly accomplished its original mission. The proof: between 1880 and 1920, the number of patents granted to individual inventors reached historic highs, reflecting an unprecedented era of popular inventiveness.

Something went off the rails in the 1970s and 1980s. The rise of technology companies and the intangible economy coincided with a profound mutation in the nature of the patent. Large corporations understood that a patent portfolio was not merely a defensive shield: it was an offensive weapon, a bargaining chip, a negotiation lever. IBM filed more than 5,000 patents per year at the height of its power, a tiny fraction of which concerned innovations it was actually developing. Qualcomm built an empire on patent licenses that today represent tens of billions of dollars in annual revenue — money paid by every smartphone manufacturer in the world, without Qualcomm itself manufacturing phones. Microsoft, Apple, and Google waged billion-dollar patent wars not to protect genuine innovations, but to block competitors or generate licensing revenue without ever producing anything concrete with those patents.

Patent trolls — entities that buy patents for the sole purpose of suing companies more productive than themselves — have become a major economic phenomenon in the United States, costing the American economy tens of billions of dollars per year in sterile litigation, according to studies from MIT and Boston University. These entities create nothing. They produce nothing. They parasitize the innovation protection system to extract value without creating any. And the preferred target of patent trolls is not Apple or Google — these giants have armies of lawyers to defend themselves. The preferred target is the small or medium-sized business that has created something real and lacks the resources for a lawsuit that will last five years and cost millions of dollars. The system designed to protect innovators has become the instrument of extortion against them.

For an individual inventor like Amadou, this drift has a concrete and devastating consequence: even if he managed to file a patent, he could never defend himself against infringement by a large corporation. Patent litigation costs on average between 1.5 and 5 million dollars per party to go to trial in the United States. In Europe, the figures are comparable. In Senegal, the reality is even bleaker: courts specializing in intellectual property are rare, lawyers competent in patent law are an even rarer commodity, and judicial delays can stretch over a decade. In other words, the current system offers real protection only to those who already have the resources to defend themselves — large corporations. For everyone else, it offers the illusion of protection and the reality of vulnerability. It is institutionalized, legal, and perfectly documented fraud.

The quality of an idea has no relationship to the financial capacity of its author to protect it. This is the fundamental injustice of the current intellectual property system. UBLinx was born from the conviction that this injustice is not inevitable — that it is technically and economically possible to build a system where protection follows the quality of the invention, not the bank balance of its author.
What UBLinx offers Amadou

In the UBLinx ecosystem, Amadou Diallo would do something radically different on the evening he closed his laptop in discouragement. He would photograph his water filter from multiple angles. He would upload his test data from the University of Dakar. He would describe in accessible terms — not in legal jargon, but in language that a competent artisan could follow — the materials used, the manufacturing steps, the operating principles. And he would submit it all to the platform. Within minutes, the UBLinx system would perform a cryptographic timestamp of the complete submission and record it on the blockchain: an immutable proof, dated to the second, that this invention existed on that day, in that form, and that it had been created by Amadou Diallo of Thiès, Senegal. This proof cannot be erased. It cannot be contradicted by a well-financed competitor. It depends on no government office, no lawyer, no national judicial system. It is inscribed in the mathematical structure of a distributed network that no one controls and no one can censor.

The next step is validation — and this is where UBLinx introduces something new to the intellectual property ecosystem. A first machine learning filter analyzes Amadou’s submission: are the physical principles of filtration consistent with known laws of physical chemistry? Are the displayed test results within plausible orders of magnitude for this type of filter medium combined in this way? Is the description sufficiently precise to be reproducible by a qualified artisan? This automatic filter does not judge the commercial value of the invention, nor its scientific elegance. It verifies that it does not violate fundamental laws of physics — thereby screening out perpetual motion machines, free energy generators, and thermodynamically impossible claims that clog every innovation submission system. If the automatic filter is satisfied, the submission goes to review by volunteer human experts from the network — chemical engineers, water treatment specialists, sustainable development practitioners in resource-limited contexts — who confirm the validity and originality of Amadou’s design.

Once validated, Amadou’s invention is protected by a smart contract — an autonomous program that runs on the blockchain and whose rules cannot be unilaterally modified by anyone, including UBLinx itself. These rules are simple and transparent: anyone who wants to manufacture and use the filter for personal use, or build up to five units for their community, does so freely and at no cost. Any organization — commercial enterprise, NGO with a significant budget, government — that wants to produce it commercially automatically triggers the payment of a royalty to Amadou Diallo. No invoices to send. No bailiffs to mobilize. No lawsuits to finance. No asymmetric negotiations with a well-staffed corporate legal department. The smart contract executes as soon as the conditions are met, and the royalty arrives in Amadou’s digital wallet as automatically as a bank transfer — but without the bank.

Honest critique: UBLinx does not solve all intellectual property problems. Smart contracts have bugs — there have been spectacular hacks in the history of decentralized finance, the most famous being the DAO hack in 2016 that resulted in a loss of 60 million dollars. Validation by volunteer experts can be slow in specialized fields, uneven depending on the quality of available volunteers, and sometimes biased toward dominant knowledge and methods in a given domain. A blockchain network can be vulnerable to concentration of computing power — what is called a 51% attack. And the legal enforcement of a royalty generated by a smart contract remains an unresolved legal problem in most jurisdictions worldwide. The question is not: is UBLinx perfect? The question is: is it dramatically better than what Amadou has today? And the answer to that question is yes, unambiguously. The total absence of protection costs infinitely more than imperfect protection.

You may have had an idea once — a product, a process, a solution to a problem that had frustrated you for a long time. What stopped you from protecting it and sharing it? Was it the cost? The delays? The legal complexity? The conviction that the idea was not «good enough»? The fear that someone would steal it before you could defend it? How many Amadous have we lost — not for lack of ideas, but for lack of access to the tools for protecting those ideas?
Why blockchain timestamping is a silent revolution

There is a fundamental difference between proving that you had an idea and proving that you had it before someone else. Patent law rests entirely on this temporal distinction — first to file wins, in the majority of modern systems. But «filing» in the current system requires considerable resources and significant delays. Between the moment Amadou conceives his filter and the moment he could obtain a patent — if he ever had the means — several years could pass. During those years, his invention is vulnerable. Blockchain timestamping radically changes this equation: proof of prior art is created within minutes, at the very moment of creation, at a cost close to zero. It is not yet a patent. But it is an incontestable evidentiary foundation that even large corporations cannot ignore in serious litigation.

The cryptographic robustness of timestamping deserves to be explained without jargon, because it is at the heart of the system’s credibility. When Amadou submits his invention, the system calculates a «digital fingerprint» (SHA-256 hash) of his document — a string of 64 characters that is mathematically unique to that precise document. If a single character of the document is changed after the fact — even a space, even a comma — the fingerprint changes completely. This fingerprint is then inscribed in a block of the blockchain, with the exact timestamp. The blockchain is a chain of blocks where each block contains the fingerprint of the previous block — retroactively modifying a block would require recalculating all subsequent blocks, which requires computing power that no one in the world possesses against a well-established blockchain. Concretely: Amadou’s record cannot be backdated, modified, or deleted. The proof exists, permanent and verifiable by anyone in the world.

This permanence has implications that go beyond simple individual protection. It creates a collective memory of human innovation — a distributed registry of who invented what and when, accessible to all, verifiable by all, belonging to no one in particular. Historians of science and technology today have fragmentary sources, often biased toward inventors from rich countries who had access to patent offices. A UBLinx registry, over decades, would become an unprecedented documentation of human creative intelligence in its real geographic and cultural diversity. This is not a secondary objective of the platform. It is an emergent consequence of its normal operation.
Chapter 2
The teenager who composes
« Creativity has no age, no passport, no distribution contract. It only needs a space where it can exist without being confiscated. »

Montreal, Rosemont borough, back bedroom. November 2031. Mei-Lin Gao, sixteen years old, wears headphones that envelop her ears like two protective shells against the rest of the world. On her screen, a music composition interface displays layers of synthesizers, electronic percussion, and a melody line she spent three weeks building note by note, fumbling, starting over, following something that resembles less a plan than a persistent intuition. She presses «play.» The music fills the room — something between Nordic minimalist electronic music and the pentatonic harmonies of her Cantonese grandparents’ musical tradition. It is strange. It is new. It is unmistakably her — not an algorithm, not a trend spotted by Spotify, not a formula proven by the labels of Los Angeles.

What is different this evening compared to what a teenage musician would have experienced in 2024 is what happens — or rather what does not happen — while she composes. No platform is recording her musical preferences to build an advertising profile that will be sold to an ad network. No algorithm is sending her compositional «mistakes» to a machine learning server belonging to a Californian company that will use them to improve its own music generation tools. The local Gen_Home artificial intelligence that sometimes helps her find harmonic transitions learns with her, for her, and stays at her home. The data from her drafts, her failed experiments, her fleeting moments of inspiration — all of it belongs to Mei-Lin, stored locally, never transmitted without her explicit and revocable consent.

We must measure what this difference truly represents, because it is less trivial than it appears. When a young musician in 2024 used any creative platform connected to the cloud — GarageBand via iCloud, Ableton Live with cloud sync, SoundCloud, Bandcamp — she was implicitly ceding a series of rights she had never explicitly granted. The terms of service of these platforms, written in dense legal English and buried in fifty-page documents that no one read, meant approximately: «By using our service, you grant us a worldwide, non-exclusive, royalty-free, transferable, and sublicensable license to use, reproduce, distribute, create derivative works from, display, and perform your content in connection with providing the service.» The musician thought she was publishing her music. She was actually signing a partial rights assignment contract with a multinational whose shareholders have no particular interest in her artistic aspirations.
Creative value and its systemic predators

The contemporary music economy illustrates with brutal clarity how digital platforms have recreated the exploitation dynamics that the record industry institutionalized in the 20th century — in an even more imbalanced version. Spotify, in 2024, paid its artists between 0.003 and 0.005 US dollars per stream. A million streams — a result that only very established artists achieve with regularity, after years of audience building — represented between 3,000 and 5,000 dollars in gross revenue. Before deducting the label’s share (which takes on average 75% for an artist under a standard contract), the digital distributor, the managers, and other intermediaries, an independent artist could hope to keep between 15 and 30% of that sum. One million streams: between 450 and 1,500 dollars in the artist’s pocket. YouTube took 45% of advertising revenue generated by artists’ videos, after unilaterally deciding which videos deserved to be monetized and which were «demonetized» for opaque algorithmic reasons. TikTok claimed usage rights over musical excerpts published on its platform, creating absurd situations where artists discovered that their own songs were being used in advertisements without their consent.

Mei-Lin knows this reality. She learned it not from an economics class but by watching her older cousin, an independent musician, accumulate streams on Spotify for five years — hundreds of thousands of streams — without being able to make a living from it. He still works at a café every morning to pay his rent and composes in the evening. She grew up with the awareness that value created and compensation received are two profoundly disconnected realities in the digital economy. This evening, after finishing her composition, she makes a conscious decision. She is going to share it — but not on Spotify, not on YouTube, not on TikTok. She is going to share it first within her closest circle of trust (her six closest friends, circle two in Gen_Home terminology), then, if the response is positive, she will decide what to do next. She is not ready to hand her work over to an algorithm without thinking first.

The next morning, all six friends have listened during the night. Three of them sent back enthusiastic voice messages. One of them, particularly intuitive about emerging musical trends, tells her with the emphasis typical of his sixteen years: «Mei-Lin, this sounds like nothing else that exists. You should really publish it properly.» She thinks it over for a day. Then she submits the composition on UBLinx. The system timestamps the work, registers it on IPFS — a distributed storage system where the file is duplicated across thousands of nodes worldwide, making its deletion practically impossible even if she herself wanted to erase it — and generates a cryptographic certificate of authenticity linked to her sovereign digital identity. The work belongs to Mei-Lin. Not to a platform. Not to a label. Not to a recommendation algorithm. To Mei-Lin Gao, sixteen years old, Rosemont, Montreal, November 17, 2031.

Osaka, Japan. Three weeks later. Kenji Takahashi, electronic music producer for seventeen years, browses the new UBLinx submissions in the category he monitors every week: «experimental electronic / non-Western tradition.» He is looking for sounds he has not yet heard, textures no one has yet combined. He comes across Mei-Lin’s composition. He listens once. He sets it aside. He returns to his other files. Ten minutes later, he comes back to it. He listens twice in a row. Something in this way of weaving pentatonic harmonies with Western subtractive synthesis creates a tension he has never encountered elsewhere — a tension that is not uncomfortable, that is rather like the tension of a well-tuned string. He sends a contact request through the platform: «Kenji Takahashi, music producer, Osaka, wishes to contact you regarding your composition November Rosemont.» Mei-Lin reads the message twice. She checks Kenji’s profile on UBLinx — his published projects, his documented collaborations, his reputation in the network. Everything is transparent. She accepts the contact.

What happens next is, in its surface structure, identical to what would have happened in 2024 — two creators make contact, discuss a collaboration. But in its details, everything is fundamentally different. Kenji wants to use Mei-Lin’s composition as the structural foundation for an album he is preparing. He proposes a revenue split. Mei-Lin — who grew up with the family council, who knows how to negotiate, who does not sign without having understood — counter-proposes. They exchange three or four messages. They reach an agreement. And when they agree, their agreement is codified in a smart contract deployed on the UBLinx blockchain: Mei-Lin receives 38% of revenue generated by any commercial exploitation of the derivative work, for a duration of ten years, after which the terms are renegotiable. This percentage is not a verbal promise. It is not a paper contract that Kenji could contest before a Japanese court that might favor him over a Canadian teenager. It is code that executes automatically. It is mathematics. It is as solid as the laws of arithmetic.

Sovereignty over one’s creations is not a privilege reserved for established artists who can afford lawyers in New York or Tokyo. It is a fundamental right that should belong to every creator from the first note, the first drawing, the first line of code written in the excitement of a discovery. UBLinx does not create this right. It finally creates the infrastructure to exercise it — so that a teenager from Rosemont can deal on equal terms with an established producer from Osaka, without an intermediary taking a commission, without an exclusivity clause she did not read, without losing what she created.

There is something deeply coherent in Mei-Lin’s journey relative to the child who grew up with the family council from Act II. That child learned early that important rules — the rules that affect what you can do with your own time, your own digital space, your own attention resources — are negotiated, discussed, co-constructed. No one unilaterally imposes terms of use on her bedroom. No one makes her sign at the bottom of a fifty-paragraph document she has not read to gain access to her own belongings. She learned that her boundaries deserve to be defined and respected, that her contributions to the family have recognized value, that decisions concerning her belong to her in proportion to her ability to understand them. This muscle — the muscle of agency, of negotiation, of sovereignty within one’s own space — is the same one Mei-Lin exercises this evening when she deals with Kenji. The architecture of sovereignty is learned at the dinner table, in the bedroom, in childhood family conversations. Then it naturally extends into the adult world.
The creative economy in the age of generative artificial intelligence

Mei-Lin’s story unfolds in a context that is evolving even faster than the digital disruption of the 2010s. Generative artificial intelligence — systems capable of composing music, generating images, writing code, producing text at a speed and cost unimaginable just a few years earlier — has created a new layer of complexity in the question of creators’ rights. When an AI system is trained on millions of musical works without the explicit permission of their authors, then generates «new» music that statistically resembles those works, who owns the result? The question is not legally resolved in most countries. It will probably not be resolved satisfactorily by existing legal systems, designed for a world where creation was fundamentally human.

UBLinx, in this context, offers something crucial: proof of human prior art that precedes any AI-generated version. If Mei-Lin registers her composition on UBLinx on November 17, 2031, and an AI system produces a similar composition on November 18, the timestamp is incontestable. Mei-Lin’s work existed first, documented, verifiable, resistant to any retroactive challenge. This ability to establish human prior art against algorithmic production will, in all likelihood, become one of the most important functions of a system like UBLinx in the decades to come — well beyond its initial mission of protection against human copying.

There is also a more subtle dimension that Mei-Lin intuitively perceives without necessarily articulating it in these terms: when she composes with Gen_Home’s local AI, her mistakes, her experiments, her idiosyncratic choices stay within her personal ecosystem. They do not feed the global model of a company that would thereby become more capable of reproducing her particular style. The boundary between assistance and parasitic learning — between a tool that serves you and a tool that studies you in order to better replace you — is maintained by the sovereign local architecture. This is not a conscious decision by Mei-Lin. It is a structural guarantee that the Gen_Home system offers by design, not by promise.
Chapter 3
The Fair Open Patent

« Between giving everything away and keeping everything, there exists a third way. It is called justice. It resembles neither generosity without compensation nor hoarding without sharing. It resembles a contract that two adults could sign with their eyes open. »

A conference room in a glass building in Basel, Switzerland. 2:37 PM. Around an oval walnut-veneered table, eight lawyers and two scientists examine a document. It concerns a patent application for a molecular compound — the modification of a surface protein that, according to the company’s internal researchers, could potentially inhibit a particularly dangerous antibiotic resistance mechanism. They are not going to develop this drug. Their pipeline is full, the return-on-investment estimates for this therapeutic pathway are deemed insufficient for current shareholders within the timeframes of the next general assembly. But a competitor, in a laboratory in Seoul, is exploring the same avenue with more modest means and a longer-term vision. The decision around the table is quick, almost administrative: they file the defensive patent. They will spend 200,000 euros to protect something they will never develop — solely so that no one else can develop it either for twenty years.

Somewhere in a laboratory in Seoul, a researcher will spend the next five years working around this patent without ever understanding why this particular angle seems legally closed. He will not know that a company in Basel locked down this pathway out of pure defensive reflex. Defensive patents are not published with a note saying «we will never develop this, we are just blocking the competition.» They look like any other patent. The innovation frontier — that space between two patent boundaries where an inventor looks for where he can go without being sued — shrinks every year, in every technological field, invisibly and silently. This is the collective cost that society pays for a system of individual innovation protection that has been distorted by interests that no longer have anything to do with innovation.
Between open source and the patent: the third way

Faced with this reality, two responses have developed over recent decades, each representing an extreme of the intellectual property spectrum. On one side, the open source movement: make everything public, all rights granted to all, let everyone use and improve freely. Innovation is maximized, diffusion is maximal, collaboration is facilitated. But the original creator is never compensated for the commercial value he created. Linux today powers the digital infrastructure of the entire planet — the servers that run Amazon, Google, Netflix, major banks, hospitals — and its thousands of volunteer contributors have never received a cent directly from these companies for this service. Linus Torvalds, the creator of the Linux kernel, works for a non-profit foundation funded by the companies that freely profit from his work. This is a form of equity — but an equity arranged after the fact, which was not in the original terms of the model.

On the other side of the spectrum, the traditional patent: lock everything down, twenty years of absolute exclusivity, let competitors invent around it or wait. Innovation is theoretically rewarded — in theory, because in practice only those who can afford the protection truly benefit from it. And the side effect is significant: the diffusion of knowledge is slowed, legal gray areas multiply, and the time inventors spend navigating existing patent portfolios is time they are not spending inventing. An OECD report estimated that in the United States alone, the costs of navigating existing patents — search costs, legal counsel, workarounds or licenses — represent a drag on innovation equivalent to several percentage points of annual GDP. The system designed to accelerate innovation effectively slows it down.

The «Fair Open Patent» model that UBLinx proposes seeks to occupy the space between these two extremes — a space that has existed philosophically for a long time, partially explored by models like Creative Commons, LGPL licenses, or the doctrine of «fair use,» but that until now lacked the technical and economic infrastructure to function at scale. The principle is simple in its statement: the invention is documented and accessible to all for study and personal replication. The creator is automatically compensated when their invention generates commercial value for others. Personal and community use (up to five copies) is free. Commercial use is subject to a royalty. It is the distinction between sharing a campfire with friends and selling electricity to a city.
Validation as a bulwark against abuse

The UBLinx system does not validate inventions based on their anticipated commercial value — it is for the market to decide whether an idea is worth something economically. It validates them based on two distinct criteria: physical coherence and claimed originality. The first layer of validation, the machine learning filter, accomplishes something precise and delimited: it verifies that the invention does not violate fundamental physical laws. An engine that claims to produce more energy than it consumes fails immediately — not because a person decided it was invalid, but because the thermodynamic equations are universal and do not negotiate with the desires of inventors. A water filter that claims to eliminate 100% of pathogens with simple materials triggers a thorough verification of the claimed mechanisms before validation. This automatic filter is not infallible: it may reject innovations that seem to defy established principles but are legitimately pushing their boundaries. An appeals procedure before human experts exists for such cases.

The second layer of human validation is more nuanced and, it must be clearly admitted, more vulnerable to bias. The volunteer experts who review submissions are human beings with their own perspectives, their own disciplinary blind spots, their own cultural prejudices. An expert in conventional engineering may miss the value of an innovation that combines approaches from different fields. A senior expert may unconsciously favor approaches that resemble what he knows and reject the unconventional approach of a self-taught inventor from West Africa. UBLinx attempts to mitigate these risks through the diversity of required validators (a minimum of three experts from different geographic regions for major technical innovations), through complete transparency of the process — every validation is public, every rejection is justified in writing, every justification can be challenged — and through a validator reputation mechanism that penalizes systematically overturned rejections. It is imperfect. Every human institution is. But public and contestable impartiality is better than the private and opaque arbitrariness of a government patent office.
The legal and technical architecture in detail

UBLinx hybrid structure: the Quebec OBNL (organisme à but non lucratif, incorporated under Quebec’s Companies Act, governed by a board of directors elected by members) holds the mission, values, and democratic governance of the platform. Important decisions — modification of validation algorithms, changes to royalty rates, modification of governance rules — require a vote of the member community. The Wyoming LLC (United States, incorporated under the Wyoming Decentralized Autonomous Organization Act of 2021) provides the legal framework compatible with crypto assets and smart contracts, in a jurisdiction that legally recognizes obligations created by DAOs and smart contracts. Invention data storage uses IPFS for distributed availability and Arweave for permanence guaranteed by a single-payment economic model. The fingerprinting system uses digital watermarking techniques robust to common transformations (compression, reformatting, cropping).

The reality of permanent storage deserves a lengthy pause, because it is one of the most profound differences between UBLinx and any innovation protection system that existed before. When Amadou submits his water filter on UBLinx, the document is not stored on a server belonging to a company that can go bankrupt, be acquired, lose its data in a fire, or unilaterally decide to delete old records for storage cost reasons. IPFS distributes the document across thousands of independent nodes around the world — each node holds a partial or complete copy of the file, addressed by its cryptographic fingerprint (hash) rather than by its physical location. There is no single server to hack, no single company to bankrupt, no single government to intimidate in order for these data to disappear. Amadou’s record will survive UBLinx itself. If anything were to happen to the platform tomorrow — bankruptcy, dissolution, attack — the proof of Amadou’s authorship would still exist, verifiable and unalterable, accessible to anyone who knows the cryptographic address of the file.

Necessary self-critique on legal limits: the legal enforcement of rights generated by smart contracts remains a largely unexplored legal territory in most judicial systems worldwide. Most judges in Paris, Nairobi, Mumbai, or Buenos Aires have no established framework for automatically recognizing obligations created by a smart contract as equivalent to those created by a contract signed before a notary. This reality means that UBLinx can offer two types of protection right now: economic protection (royalties flow automatically without needing to go to court) and evidentiary protection (authorship is incontestable before any court that accepts digital evidence). Full legal protection — the ability to prosecute a commercial infringement in any jurisdiction with a smart contract as the main piece of evidence — will take time and an evolution of the international legal framework. That time is probably measured in decades, not years.
The cooperative model applied to the validation of ideas

Peer community validation is the element of the UBLinx model that draws the most skepticism from lawyers and innovation economists. «Validation by volunteers? That can’t hold at scale.» It is a legitimate critique that deserves an honest answer. Volunteer validation exists and works at large scale in domains where the stakes are just as high as intellectual property: Wikipedia validates millions of articles in multiple languages with an average accuracy that exceeds that of many professional encyclopedias. Stack Overflow solves millions of technical problems through a community of volunteer developers. Peer-reviewed scientific journals operate entirely on the unpaid work of fellow researchers. Volunteer validation works when it is well structured, when the rules are clear and public, and when contributors find value — reputational, intellectual, or communal — in their participation.

UBLinx’s specific challenge is the diversity of technical domains to cover: a water filter in Senegal and a musical composition in Montreal require radically different validation competencies. The solution the architecture proposes is specialization and decentralization of validator pools: each technical domain has its own validator community, with its own criteria and its own consensus thresholds. An inventor in Senegal in the water treatment field does not wait for validation from an electronic music expert in Tokyo — his file goes directly to the pool of water engineering and sustainable development experts. Specialization improves quality, and geographic decentralization reduces the cultural biases of a mono-centric system.

What motivates volunteer validators? The question deserves to be asked frankly, because the sustainability of any cooperative system depends on the answer. In the most durable cooperative projects — Linux, Wikipedia, credit unions — contributors find several types of value simultaneously: use value (they themselves use what they build), reputational value (their expertise is visible and recognized in the community), missionary value (they contribute to something greater than themselves that they believe is just). UBLinx aims to activate all three dimensions: validators benefit from reciprocity when their own submissions go through validation, their expertise is documented and visible, and they participate in building a system they consider more just than the alternative. This is not a guarantee of success. It is a design aligned with what we know about motivation in durable cooperative systems.
Chapter 4
The network of sovereign households

« Connection without submission. Community without fusion. Solidarity without dissolution. This was the original dream of the Internet. It is not dead. It waits — in the forgotten protocols, in the decentralized architectures that no one had any interest in funding until now. »

Quebec, suburb of Quebec City. December 2032, Christmas Eve. The Tremblay family is at home. Their immediate neighbors, the Garniers — a family of Martinican origin who arrived in Quebec twelve years ago — are two houses away. At the other end of the street, the Okafor-Lapointe family, whose mother is a nurse and father is a civil engineer, recently installed their Gen_Home system. These three families know each other with the cordiality of good neighbors — they like each other, greet each other, do small winter favors, sometimes exchange homemade dishes — but their bonds are those of ordinary neighbors, warm without being intimate. This Christmas Eve, their children play together in the snow of the front yard, and their Gen_Home systems are silently connected via the UBLinx network — each according to the parameters its family has set, not according to those of a central platform.

What this connection transmits between the three houses is precisely delimited by explicit choices. The weather sensors of the three properties — outdoor temperature, relative humidity, snow accumulation on roofs, wind speed at garden level — feed a micro weather network that allows each household to better plan winter maintenance. The three families had mutually consented to this sharing during installation, through an interface that had explicitly asked them, checkbox by checkbox: «Do you want to share your outdoor temperature data? Your electricity consumption data? Your presence data?» Mrs. Tremblay specifically excluded her electricity consumption data from sharing — she prefers to keep her habits private. Mr. Okafor-Lapointe included his indoor air quality data because he wants to contribute to research on domestic pollution in the neighborhood. Mrs. Garnier has shared nothing at all for now — she is observing, learning, and will decide when she feels ready. Each person chooses. No one controls the others’ choices.

This domestic scenario contains in miniature the entire political philosophy of the UBLinx network: connection is always opt-in, never opt-out. Shared data is explicitly defined by each participant, not implicitly siphoned by the platform that then decides its use. The architecture is peer-to-peer — the three houses communicate directly with each other through a distributed protocol, without passing through a central UBLinx server that could aggregate, analyze, or resell this data. If UBLinx disappeared tomorrow as an organization — bankruptcy, dissolution, acquisition by an entity with different intentions — the three houses would continue to communicate via the distributed protocol, because their connection depends on no single intermediary whose disappearance would sever the links. This is the fundamental architectural difference between this network and what Nest, Amazon Ring, or Google Home offered a decade earlier: those systems connected homes through the company’s server. Remove the company, and all connections fall. In the UBLinx model, the organization is an initial facilitator, not a permanent bottleneck.
The Quebec cooperative tradition and its digital mutation

To understand why this architecture has particular resonance in Quebec, we must briefly retrace the history of a financial institution born in the misery of a Laurentian winter. In 1900, Alphonse Desjardins — journalist, parliamentary stenographer, an ordinary man driven by an extraordinary indignation at the usurious rates charged to poor workers — founded in Lévis the first North American credit union. His observation was simple and devastating: banks lent to the rich and ignored the poor. Workers could not access credit to buy a cow, repair a barn, survive a bad harvest, or finance a child’s education. His answer: let the workers themselves lend to each other, according to their own rules, in their own institution that they collectively own and democratically govern. A century and a half later, Desjardins is the sixth largest financial group in Canada and one of the strongest cooperative institutions in the Western world. Not despite its cooperative principles, but because of them — the loyalty of members who see themselves in their institution’s values created a stability that short-term profit maximization could never have generated.

Quebec’s history is strewn with these collective institutions born of necessity and mistrust of external powers that did not understand local needs. Agricultural cooperatives that allowed farmers to negotiate collectively with industrial buyers. Mutual fire insurance companies that covered wooden houses in rural regions that commercial insurers refused. Cégeps and the university network designed as accessible public service institutions rather than commercial enterprises. The reduced-contribution daycare network — of which Cynthia Richelieu, assistant director at CPE Chez-Nous, knows the tensions and virtues from the inside — which transformed access to early childhood care from a privilege into a right. All these institutions share the same fundamental conviction, rooted in Quebec political culture since the Quiet Revolution: certain goods and services are too important to be left solely to the logic of private profit, and communities have the capacity and the legitimacy to govern themselves in these domains.

The traditional cooperative had an inescapable geographic limitation: its members had to know each other, share a territory, gather for a general assembly in the same room. This limitation was both a strength — it anchored the institution in a real community, with familiar faces and shared responsibilities — and a weakness — it made extension beyond the reach of direct social bonds impossible. The blockchain resolves this limitation in a way that Desjardins could not have imagined but would probably have applauded: it enables trust without prior acquaintance between members. Participants in a UBLinx network in Thiès, Montreal, Osaka, and Helsinki do not need to know each other personally to trust each other within the framework of their transactions. The system’s rules are transparent and identical for all, commitments are automatically enforced by code that no one unilaterally controls, and each participant’s reputation is visible to all in the form of an immutable history of past behaviors. This is the credit union at planetary scale — with the same logic of mutual trust pooling, but without the geographic constraint that limited its reach.
Community resilience as an emergent common good

The three families on our Quebec City street do not share only weather data. In the more developed version of the network, aggregated and anonymized health data — what epidemiological trends are emerging at the hyper-local level, what symptoms are circulating in the neighborhood by season, what preventive interventions correlate with better outcomes — can feed a community health dashboard that each family can access, and that the neighborhood family doctor can consult with the explicit and revocable permission of the residents. No one has to reveal their own individual medical situation. Anonymized aggregation by privacy-preserving computation algorithms suffices to detect an unusual rise in respiratory symptoms three weeks before it becomes visible in regional public health statistics — allowing families with young children or immunocompromised individuals to take preventive action.

The crucial aspect — the one that fundamentally distinguishes this network from previous «smart city» or «connected health» approaches — is what I call «connection without submission.» When a family joined Amazon Alexa or Google Home, it accepted a structurally asymmetric relationship: it gave its data, its habits, its behavioral profile, its most intimate private space, and in exchange received convenience services. The transaction was real but the consent was illusory, because the two parties did not have the same ability to exit the relationship. The family could theoretically uninstall Alexa. But its data was already on Amazon’s servers, replicated across dozens of data centers, aggregated into behavioral profiles sold to dozens of advertisers and partner companies. The digital past does not uninstall. In the UBLinx network, connection implies no data cession to any unconsented third party. The Okafor-Lapointe family can leave the network tomorrow, withdraw its data (it is at their home, not on a remote server), and nothing will remain in any system that would allow anyone to infer that this family participated in the network. Sovereignty includes the right to leave without a trace.

A network of sovereign households is not a technological utopia. It is the application to the digital age of a principle that Quebecers and other peoples have practiced for centuries: we are stronger together than isolated, provided we remain free to govern ourselves. The 21st-century credit union is not a bank. It is a network of sovereign data where each node retains its full decision-making power, and where collective value emerges from voluntary cooperation between sovereignties, not from their absorption into a centralized super-sovereignty.

It is important to honestly name what this network is not, and what it should not be asked to be. A network of sovereign households is not a community in the strong sense — the Tremblay, Garnier, and Okafor-Lapointe families share data, not necessarily deep values, common political projects, or a shared vision of the common good. Such a network can perfectly well connect people with diametrically opposed opinions on politics, religion, and economics, who happen to share the same street and the same pragmatic interest in the sustainable management of their domestic resources. This is, in fact, both its strength and its limitation: it does not require ideological homogeneity, which makes it universal. But it does not build community in the political sense, which means it cannot replace the spaces of democratic deliberation where values are collectively negotiated. It is a tool of sovereignty, not a substitute for politics.
The Internet was supposed to be this: the story of a diverted promise

To fully understand what the UBLinx network seeks to rebuild, we must briefly revisit the history of what the original Internet was supposed to be — and what it became. In 1969, ARPANET — the precursor of the Internet — was designed as a military network capable of surviving nuclear attacks thanks to its decentralized architecture: no single node whose destruction would bring down the entire network. In the 1980s and 1990s, when this network was opened to universities and then to the public, the pioneers of the civilian Internet — Tim Berners-Lee with the World Wide Web, Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn with TCP/IP — shared a vision of a fundamentally peer-to-peer, decentralized, ownerless network, where each node had the same architectural dignity as the others. Intelligence was supposed to be at the edges of the network (with users), not at its center.

This vision was betrayed gradually and methodically during the 2000s and 2010s — not by a conscious and centralized decision, but by the natural effect of economies of scale and the logic of platforms with network effects. Each additional user on Facebook increased the value of Facebook for all other users, which attracted new users, which further increased the value, in a self-reinforcing cycle that inevitably led to massive concentration. By 2024, five companies — Alphabet (Google), Meta (Facebook), Amazon, Apple, and Microsoft — controlled the overwhelming majority of internet traffic, personal data storage, cloud services, and recommendation algorithms that decided what people saw, read, and bought. The decentralized network had become a five-pointed star — five centers of unprecedented power in the history of human communication.

UBLinx does not claim to «fix the Internet» — a task whose scale far exceeds the ambitions of a Quebec project in its conceptual phase. It claims to create, in a specific and delimited domain, an infrastructure that honors the original vision of the network: peer-to-peer, decentralized, with no single node of control. If this model works for the protection of innovations and the connection of sovereign households, it can serve as a proof of feasibility for other domains. Each successful demonstration of practicable decentralization reduces the scope of the argument that says centralization is inevitable, natural, necessary. Centralization is not natural. It is the result of design choices and incentive structures. Other choices are possible.
Chapter 5
Ethical monetization

« To invent is to create value for the world. To be compensated for that value is justice. To seize that value without any return for its creator is the legalized theft we have gradually normalized to the point of no longer seeing it. »

My basement in Saint-Dominique. Until quite recently, it was a mess — tons of uncompleted projects, accumulated in a room where you had to follow a difficult and precarious path through bins of tools and the girls’ old Halloween costumes to reach my desk. At the end of that path: three screens, my terminal, from which I answer clients to help them install our elevator controllers. I have been doing telephone technical support for JRT Ascenseurs for more than ten years. On the corner of the desk, an old water bottle cut in half with eight relay coils glued around it with hot glue — a prototype of something that never got past the intuition stage. My walls are covered with annotated documents, diagrams, birthday cards my daughters made for me, family photos. And on the corner of my desk, an old red binder — my technical support bible, dog-eared from ten years of daily use. This is where it all started. Not from a research lab, not from a university, not from a technology incubator. From a cluttered basement in Saint-Dominique, between a cold cup of coffee and memories of my daughters. Two hundred innovations documented in my notebooks, not yet filed — solutions to concrete industrial problems, optimized processes, tools reimagined for specific contexts. How many of these innovations have been lost — silently taken up by competitors, rediscovered by better-funded teams, simply forgotten because the context was not yet ripe when they emerged — without my ever knowing?

I am not a romantic inventor cut off from the commercial world. Ten years of industrial technical support at JRT Ascenseurs taught me contractual realities, the dynamics of the professional world, and the value of work well done. I have seen from the inside how the industry works — its strengths, its inequalities, its blind spots. But I am also fundamentally honest in my assessment of the situation: for an individual inventor in Quebec, even with years of experience and more than two hundred innovations documented in notebooks, the current intellectual property protection system is chronically unequal, structurally slow, and prohibitively expensive for protecting each individual innovation. None of my innovations has ever been patented. Not because they did not intellectually deserve it, but because I simply cannot afford it. A US national patent costs between 15,000 and 50,000 dollars. An international patent, several times more. For an independent technician in Quebec doing elevator technical support from his basement, those figures are prohibitive. Two hundred innovations in my notebooks, and not a single patent to protect them. This is exactly the problem UBLinx seeks to solve.
The five-copy rule: the philosophy of a precise boundary

The rule that defines the boundary between personal use and commercial use in the UBLinx system deserves a detailed explanation, because it is not arbitrary and its choice reveals a coherent social philosophy. Why five copies? Why not ten? Why not just one? The reasoning starts from an anthropological observation about the natural structure of human relationships. Human beings function in trust circles of different sizes — the family nucleus (3–5 people), the close friends circle (5–15 people), the neighborhood community (50–150 people, what anthropologists call the «Dunbar number»). Exchanges within the family nucleus and close friends are fundamentally different from commercial transactions: you lend your car to your best friend without charging mileage. You share your grandmother’s recipe with your brother-in-law without demanding royalties. You build a tool for your immediate neighbor without drawing up a service contract.

Five copies represents approximately this threshold — the number beyond which you begin to leave the circle of direct personal acquaintance and enter wider distribution. Building a water pump for yourself, for your mother, for your brother-in-law, for two immediate neighbors: that is proximity mutual aid, the normal social fabric, something that taxing would be both technically impossible and socially regressive. Building ten pumps to sell at the weekly market: that is a commercial activity, a transformation of Amadou’s creative value into revenue for you, without having asked his permission or offered him a share of your profits. The line between five and six is not a precise natural boundary — nature is continuous, human categories are discrete. But it is a boundary drawn with real social coherence, not an arithmetic whim.

For nations of the Global South, where access to foreign innovations has long been constrained either by inaccessible patents (too expensive to license legally) or by their total absence of protection (allowing copying without compensation), the five-copy rule has a particularly transformative reach. An artisan in Cambodia can freely build five units of Amadou’s filtration pump for his village, using locally available materials, adapting the dimensions to specific needs. He can improve the design, adapt the materials to local resources, and if he finds a significant improvement, submit it on UBLinx under his own name as a derived innovation — with attribution to Amadou as the original source. The flow of innovation circulates in both directions, horizontally, between peers, without the mediation of a patent office in Geneva that has never visited a Cambodian village and does not understand its specific constraints.
Economic models in comparison

segments.text: To concretely situate the UBLinx proposal within the landscape of existing approaches, four models deserve direct comparison.

offers the strongest protection (20 years of legal exclusivity in signatory countries) but at the price of extremely limited access: 15,000 to 50,000 dollars minimum for a US national patent, two to five years of delay, hermetic legal language, inaccessible without a specialized lawyer.

is universal, free, and simple, but contains no mechanism for direct compensation of the author for commercial uses: a CC-BY-SA license allows any company to manufacture your invention on an industrial scale and owe you legally nothing.
maximizes diffusion and collective improvement, but systematically decouples value creation from value capture.

proposes a fourth way: free diffusion for personal and community use, automatic compensation for commercial use, decentralized validation of authorship, permanent storage of proof. None of these models is perfect. Each responds to different needs.

text: The concrete question UBLinx poses to its users is this: what is your objective with this innovation? If you want maximum legal protection within the existing judicial system, the traditional patent remains the best answer — provided you can afford it. If you want maximum diffusion without any restriction, Creative Commons or open source are coherent choices. If you want free diffusion for personal uses and automatic compensation for commercial uses, with incontestable and permanent proof of authorship, UBLinx proposes an infrastructure for that. These three options are not mutually exclusive: some inventors may choose to submit certain innovations on UBLinx and traditionally patent others, depending on the nature of the innovation and the resources available.

The ecosystem effect that emerges when a network like UBLinx reaches critical mass is difficult to model with statistical precision, but its intuition is striking. If every individual innovation that would once have vanished into anonymity or been absorbed without attribution by a more powerful actor is now documented, timestamped, validated, and potentially compensated for its commercial uses, the signal sent to the population of latent inventors — all those individuals who innovate in their field but do not know how to protect and monetize their discoveries — is fundamental: the effort of inventing is worth something concrete, not just something symbolic. It will be recognized. It could generate a modest but real and automatic income. This signal, which did not exist for the individual inventor without resources, could trigger a wave of distributed innovation whose amplitude and diversity would be unprecedented in the economic history of humanity.

Ethical monetization is not an oxymoron. It is the recognition that economic value and social value are not in fundamental opposition — they become so only when value capture systems are structurally disconnected from value creation systems. UBLinx proposes to reconnect them, directly, automatically, without an intermediary that pays itself by taking the lion’s share of the flow. Technology does not create this connection — it simply makes it practicable at a global scale for the first time.

There is a personal dimension to all this that I do not dodge. I think about my daughters, Jordane and Olivia. I think about the world in which they will have to create, contribute, innovate. The system in which I invented for years was imperfect but navigable for someone who understood its mechanisms and knew how to be patient. Jordane and Olivia will live in a world where generative artificial intelligence will have considerably lowered the technical cost of creation — anyone will be able to produce code, music, designs, scientific texts at a speed and quantity previously unimaginable. That world will be wonderful for the diversity of what can be created and expressed. It will be potentially catastrophic for the rights of individual creators if systems like UBLinx are not in place to distinguish the original author from the aggregator, the creator from the exploiter, the added value from mere replication. It is for that world that UBLinx is designed — so that Jordane and Olivia can create in an environment where their contributions truly belong to them.
The question of fair price: between extraction and exploitation

When we talk about «ethical monetization,» we inevitably touch upon the question of the fair price — a question that economic philosophy has debated since Antiquity and that modern markets do not resolve; they displace it. The fair price of an innovation is not its production cost — in the economy of ideas, the marginal cost of reproducing an idea tends toward zero. Nor is it its short-term market value — markets systematically underestimate disruptive innovations at the time of their creation (no one wanted to fund Xerox PARC, where the graphical PC and Ethernet were invented) and overestimate them during bubble phases (the internet valuations of 1999). The fair price of an innovation is something harder to measure: its contribution to human well-being, distributed across time and space, often impossible to evaluate at the moment of creation.

UBLinx does not claim to solve the question of the fair price — it is a philosophical and economic problem for which no perfect solution exists. It proposes something more modest and more practicable: that the person who creates the value receives an automatic and non-negotiable share of the commercial revenue generated by that value. Not the entirety — the companies that industrialize, distribute, and maintain an invention add real value and deserve to be compensated. But the share that returns to the original creator must not depend on their ability to negotiate against a corporate legal department, nor be reduced to nothing by exclusivity contracts signed from a position of weakness. The royalty codified in immutable code is a form of minimum wage for innovation — a floor below which creators cannot be pushed, regardless of the power differential between them and their licensees.

We must also name the positive effect on creative risk-taking. Innovation is fundamentally a high-risk activity: most innovations fail, in the sense that they do not find a market or arrive too early. In the current system, the individual inventor bears nearly all of this risk — investing time, energy, sometimes savings, and if the innovation fails commercially, receiving nothing. If it succeeds and was not protected, someone else takes the value. The prospect of automatic proportional compensation — even modest — for every commercial use of successful innovations changes the risk calculus for the individual inventor. It does not eliminate risk, but it increases the expected gain in case of success. This is sufficient to unlock creative energy that uncompensated risk had been blocking until now.
Chapter 6
What distributed trust builds first

« Trust is not a commodity distributed from a center to grateful peripheries. It is a property that emerges from repeated interactions between equals who respect rules they themselves established, and who can freely exit without losing what they brought in. »

Thiès, Senegal. March 2035. Four years have passed since the evening when Amadou Diallo searched «how to protect an invention in Senegal» and closed his laptop with the helplessness of a man who sees something precious being taken from him with nothing he can do about it. Tonight, he is sitting at the same wooden table in his workshop — which is no longer quite the same, expanded by a side room, equipped with a lathe and a press that the first royalties made it possible to purchase. A metal shelf holds about fifteen water filters in the process of being manufactured. A second workbench hosts a solar pump prototype he is developing in collaboration with an engineer in Ghana — Emmanuel Acheampong, whom he has never met in person but whose calculation notebooks he knows as well as his own. Their collaboration exists entirely in digital space, mediated by UBLinx and sovereign communication tools.

Amadou’s original filter is now used in forty-seven villages in the Thiès department, most of them installed by local artisans under the five-copy rule — free community use, costing nothing to Amadou or to those communities. This is exactly what he wanted for his design. Three humanitarian organizations have contacted Amadou for larger-scale production — hundreds, potentially thousands of units, for development programs in other regions of Senegal and Mali. One of them has already regularized its commercial use through a UBLinx smart contract, after a short and transparent negotiation on the royalty rate. The other two are in discussions. On his computer — now without a cracked screen, replaced thanks to the first royalties — Amadou monitors a simple dashboard. It shows the number of active licenses, royalties received this month and cumulatively, and a history of integrity checks. It is not a fortune. But it is regular. It is automatic. And it is just.

Amadou’s story illustrates something that innovation economists have long theorized but that existing institutions have not yet managed to operationalize: the direct compensation of the individual inventor changes innovation behaviors in deep and rapid ways. When Amadou receives his first royalties, he does not consume them entirely. He reinvests a significant portion in the new solar pump project — a natural extension of his filtration expertise into pumping energy. He hires a local apprentice — a nineteen-year-old who had dropped out of school for lack of funds — and teaches him manufacturing techniques. He documents his processes more carefully, because he now knows that documentation has concrete value: it accelerates UBLinx validation, it allows licensees to reproduce his inventions correctly, and it facilitates collaborations like the one with Emmanuel in Ghana. The virtuous cycle between reward and innovation is not theoretical. It is visible in Amadou’s workshop in Thiès.
The restored social contract between creators and society

The central question of this act — intellectual and social sovereignty — is not fundamentally a question of technology. The blockchain, smart contracts, IPFS, machine learning algorithms for validation — these are tools. Sophisticated, technically elegant, powerful tools within their domain — but tools, whose value is entirely defined by the human uses and social structures they enable. The fundamental question is that of the social contract between creators and society: what does society owe those who generate the innovations that allow the collective to progress? And what do creators owe the society that made their creation possible — by funding the education that trained them, the physical and digital infrastructure that connects them, the decades of accumulated knowledge from generations of inventors and researchers they necessarily build upon, even when they innovate most radically?

The current intellectual property system’s answer to this question is asymmetric and partially fraudulent. It says: society protects the inventor for twenty years of exclusivity, after which the invention falls into the public domain for the benefit of all. But this answer only accounts for a fraction of reality — the fraction of inventions that were patented, meaning those whose authors had the means for protection. Amadou’s inventions that could never be patented? They fell into the public domain the very day of their creation — freely, for everyone, including for companies that could exploit them commercially on a large scale — without Amadou receiving anything. The system claims to protect creators, but in practice it only protects those who can afford the protection. The others involuntarily and freely subsidize the public domain of well-capitalized companies. It is a wealth transfer from poor to rich, legalized and systematized.

UBLinx proposes a different answer, articulated around three precise philosophical distinctions. Fundamental scientific knowledge — the laws of thermodynamics, the principles of molecular biology, mathematical theorems — belongs to all of humanity and cannot be privatized. The concrete and original implementation of an idea — the transformation of general principles into a specific solution, materialized by an inventor or a team — creates a right to compensation for the commercial uses of that specific solution. Personal and community use remains free, because proximity exchanges and direct mutual aid are the fabric of normal social life, and taxing them would be regressive and impossible. These three distinctions create a philosophically coherent space between the all-public of open source and the all-private of the patent. It is what the patent system would have wanted to be, if its 19th-century architects could have anticipated the digital revolution and the global economy of ideas it created.
What this network does not yet solve: the honest limits

The distributed trust that UBLinx builds has real limits that it would be dishonest not to name clearly. The first is the classic problem of initial adoption, what economists call the «egg and chicken problem»: a trust network only has value if enough people participate so that the advantages of connection exceed the costs of adoption. UBLinx in its 2035 scenario has reached critical mass in certain sectors — technical innovations in francophone and developing countries, independent music and digital arts in certain regions — but remains marginal in other sectors with high regulatory intensity such as pharmacology or aeronautics. An inventor in the pharmaceutical sector would not yet benefit from UBLinx protection with the same solidity as Amadou in water treatment — simply because the network of specialized expert validators in medicinal chemistry is not yet sufficiently developed, and because regulatory authorities like the FDA do not yet recognize UBLinx certifications as proof of authorship in their approval processes.

The second limit is structural and linked to the very nature of human knowledge: UBLinx is more effective at protecting innovations that can be documented, described, and verified textually or visually than at protecting innovations that reside in tacit know-how and non-formalizable embodied experience. A master cheesemaker whose genius lies in reading the texture of the curd at a precise moment of production, a sculptor whose value is in the way his hand adjusts pressure to the grain of the stone, an engineer whose expertise consists of listening to the sounds of a machine and diagnosing problems invisible to instruments — these forms of knowledge cannot be deposited on a blockchain. They live in bodies, in hands, in accumulated experience that no one else can transcribe in the master’s stead. Technical documentation formalizes one part of human knowledge; it necessarily leaves another part in the register of direct and personal transmission.

The third limit is political, and it may be the most serious. A distributed and decentralized trust network represents, by nature, a threat to actors who derive their economic and political power from the centralization of intellectual property. The large technology companies that built empires on defensive patent portfolios, the government patent offices that generate substantial revenue from their procedures, the law firms specializing in intellectual property whose high fees are guaranteed by the complexity of the current system, the entertainment industries that built entire business models on capturing the creative value of artists — all these actors have direct and substantial interests in thwarting UBLinx’s success. They have the resources to lobby legislators, to finance creative legal challenges, to create alternatives superficially similar to UBLinx without its fundamental sovereignty. The history of decentralization attempts in finance, media, and energy shows that established interests have a remarkable capacity to reabsorb disruptive innovations — either by buying them or by regulating them into ineffectiveness.

Final and honest critique: an essay that presents UBLinx as a solution to the injustice of intellectual property would be incomplete if it did not say this clearly and without euphemism — UBLinx is a conceptual architecture with solid internal coherence and real technical feasibility, but it is not yet a production system tested at large scale in social and economic reality. The dragons waiting in the details of implementation — smart contract bugs, governance tensions between members with divergent interests, organized resistance from established systems, blockchain scalability issues, validation difficulties in specialized domains — are real and non-trivial. This book does not promise that UBLinx will work exactly as described. It argues that the architecture is philosophically just, that the problem it seeks to solve is real and urgent, and that it is better to build something imperfect and just than to continue indefinitely enduring a system that is imperfect and fundamentally unjust.
The bridge to what comes next: the inevitable tension

The trust network we have described in this act — between inventors who protect their creations, between creators who negotiate their rights, between sovereign households that share data without submitting — rests on a fundamental hypothesis that we have not yet examined: that communications between the nodes of this network are secure, private, non-interceptable by unconsented third parties. That Amadou can communicate with Emmanuel in Ghana without that communication being monitored by intelligence services, commercial competitors, or governments that might have interests in knowing about his projects. That Mei-Lin can negotiate with Kenji in Osaka without their conversation being exploited by an advertising algorithm or scrutinized by a curious tax authority. That the data shared between the three families in Quebec City stays between those three families and does not silently flow to unconsented third parties.

This hypothesis raises a question that every decentralized system must honestly confront, without dodging it with reassuring formulas: sovereign communications protect the innocent — the Amadous, the Mei-Lins, the Davids — but they also protect, as an inevitable consequence of their architecture, malicious actors who know their communications cannot be monitored. This is the fundamental tension between individual privacy and collective security that every free society must constantly negotiate, without any final and definitive resolution. It has no technical solution. It has only political compromises, always temporary, always contestable, always revisable as context changes. What the next act of this book proposes to explore is not a solution to this tension, but a way to name it honestly and to live with it without resolving it through the arbitrary suppression of one of its terms.

Because the easiest solution — surveilling everyone to catch the bad actors, sacrificing everyone’s privacy for each person’s security — has already been tried. It has a name that 21st-century historians associate with an era of techno-political naïveté: mass surveillance. Its track record, over the three decades during which it was practiced at scale by liberal democracies, is eloquent in what it did not accomplish. Major terrorist threats were not significantly reduced by the mass surveillance of entire populations’ communications — the major attacks of the 2010s and 2020s generally involved actors already known to intelligence services, whose intensive surveillance had not been sufficient to prevent their actions.
