In contrast, mass surveillance created a permanent apparatus for observing ordinary populations — the Amadous, the Mei-Lins, the Davids — with all the potential for abuse in political systems that history teaches us never to presume definitively stable and irreversibly free.

If you had invented something that could change lives — a tool, a process, a medication, a song, an algorithm that solves a real problem for real people — how would you protect it today? How much would it cost you, in time, money, and administrative energy? If you had neither the money nor the time nor the access to the necessary lawyers — what would your idea be worth in ten years, and to whom would it belong de facto, even though it belongs to you by right?
Amadou’s cycle: a living proof of concept

Amadou’s story in this act follows a structure that economists recognize as a «virtuous innovation cycle» — but one that the current system systematically blocks for actors without resources. The revenue from a first innovation allows the financing of a second. The second generates a reputation that attracts collaborators. The collaborators bring complementary skills that increase the quality of subsequent innovations. The subsequent innovations generate more royalties. This cycle — well documented in the biographies of the great inventors of the industrial era — is not reserved for solitary geniuses. It is available to anyone who has a first good idea and a system that allows them to capture enough of its value to reinvest. This is precisely what Amadou is experiencing in his workshop in Thiès in 2035: not a fairy tale, but the ordinary economic logic of the virtuous cycle, finally accessible to those who had been excluded from it by the barriers of the traditional system.

What is remarkable about Amadou’s story is also what it says about the relationship between individual innovation and the collective good in the UBLinx model. His water filter is now used in forty-seven villages — thousands of people accessing safer water. This immense collective impact occurred without Amadou giving up his rights, without having to choose between his personal interest and the common good. The two coexisted. The families who built their own filters under the five-copy rule paid nothing — that was their right. The organizations that commercialized the filter on a large scale paid royalties — that was their obligation. The model did not force an impossible choice. It created the conditions for diffusion and compensation to naturally coexist, each in its appropriate sphere.

This coexistence is precisely what the debate between «intellectual property versus the common good» presented as impossible for decades. Defenders of the traditional patent and advocates of open source clashed in a false dilemma: either you protect the inventor and block diffusion, or you favor diffusion and sacrifice the inventor. UBLinx shows — or at least proposes an architecture that would show, once implemented — that this dilemma is not logically inevitable. It is the product of specific technical and institutional constraints of the 20th-century patent system, not a universal economic law. Change the technical and institutional constraints, and you change the possibilities. This is, in its simplest formulation, the thesis of this act.
Epilogue: What the child of the family council intuitively understands

Let us recall the child from Act II — the one who grew up in a house where important rules were negotiated around a table, where sovereignty over one’s own digital space was a right exercised from the age of understanding what it concretely meant. That child, now grown up, enters the economy of creation with a reflex that their peers raised in traditional systems have not developed: they know that their creations belong to them, that the rules of sharing are theirs to define, that the value their creations generate for others should return to them in proportion to their original contribution. They do not know this because they were given an intellectual property law course at twelve. They know it because they exercised the muscle of sovereignty in the concrete and daily context of their family life, since childhood, in situations where the stakes were real but the consequences of a mistake remained learnable.

This is perhaps the most underestimated contribution of the Conscience Souveraine project as a whole — not the algorithms, not the smart contracts, not the blockchain protocols. The deepest and most lasting contribution is pedagogical: by building spaces of sovereignty at the household scale, by accustoming children from the earliest age to the grammar of agency and informed consent, we are shaping individuals who intuitively carry the logic of sovereignty into every one of their economic and social acts in adulthood. Individuals who do not unconsciously sign fifty-page terms of use without reading them. Individuals who ask «who owns this and under what rules?» before participating in a system. Individuals who know that their creations have a value that deserves protection and that this protection is a right, not a privilege conditioned on the size of their bank account. These individuals form the trust network. Not the protocols. The protocols merely make practicable at a global scale what sovereign individuals have decided to build together.

Yoshua Bengio, the Montreal artificial intelligence researcher whose public alarm about the existential risks of AI helped steer the thinking behind this project, articulated something important that popular debates about AI tend to obscure: the main danger is not that machines become conscious and autonomously decide to harm us. The main danger is that non-conscious but extremely powerful and increasingly capable machines are used by human actors — authoritarian states, corporations seeking monopoly positions, individuals with power concentration agendas — to redistribute social, economic, and political power in a way unprecedented in human history. The decentralized trust network — UBLinx, Gen_Home, the digital sovereignty protocols — is not a technical answer to a technical problem. It is a political and social answer to a political and social threat: the reconcentration of power in the digital age, under the guise of efficiency, security, and innovation, by actors who benefit precisely from the centralization they propose as a universal solution.

Intellectual and social sovereignty is not a luxury reserved for wealthy nations, well-educated individuals, or established entrepreneurs. It is the precondition for all authentic innovation — innovation that emerges freely from human imagination without having to ask permission from a system that did not fund it, that circulates equitably between creators and users without an intermediary capturing the bulk of the value, and that translates into real progress primarily for those who need it most. The trust network is the infrastructure of this sovereignty. It is conceptually coherent, technically feasible, economically justified. It remains to be built — and those who will build it will not be the companies that benefit from the status quo, but the inventors, creators, communities, and individuals who have everything to gain from a more just system.

« Distributed trust does not eliminate risks. It redistributes them more equitably among all participants in a network, rather than concentrating them on the most vulnerable nodes — those that lack the size, resources, or connections to protect themselves alone in the current system. This is the promise of the trust network. It is also its limitation — it does not eliminate inequalities, it redistributes them. Above all, it is its necessity — because the world we live in is not the one we deserve to live in, and building that other world begins by honestly naming the distance between the two. » — David Berthelotte
Note on the current status of UBLinx

Throughout this act, we have described UBLinx in the future and conditional tenses — Amadou «would,» Mei-Lin «would submit,» the families of Quebec City «would be connected.» This temporal consistency is not rhetorical: it reflects the reality of the project. UBLinx, at the time these pages are written, is a conceptual architecture whose technological components are all individually available and proven — blockchain, smart contracts, IPFS, Arweave, machine learning for validation, digital fingerprinting — but whose assembly into a coherent, governed, accessible, and durable system has not yet taken place. Identifying this gap between vision and reality is not an admission of failure. It is the precondition of any honest project: knowing where we are in order to know where we are going.

The roadmap leading from concept to reality passes through several stages that honesty compels us to name. First, the development of the community validation protocol in one or two pilot domains — water treatment and low-resource agricultural innovations are natural candidates, because these domains have expert communities motivated by a clear social mission and inventors who have nothing to lose by trying a new approach. Then, the implementation of hybrid legal structures in the most favorable jurisdictions. Then the progressive growth of the network of validators and inventors, domain by domain, region by region. Finally, integration with national legal systems as the legal recognition of smart contracts progresses across different jurisdictions. This is not a five-year plan. It is probably the work of a generation.

But projects that change lasting structures take the time they take. Desjardins took forty years to become a major institution in Quebec. Linux took twenty years to become the invisible infrastructure of the global digital economy. Wikipedia took fifteen years to become the main source of encyclopedic information for the majority of humanity. What these projects had in common at the start: a clear vision of what was unjust in the existing system, a coherent proposal for how to make it more just, and an initial community sufficiently motivated to start building without waiting for everything to be perfect. UBLinx has the first two ingredients. The third — the community — is what this book seeks, among other things, to help assemble.

Would you be willing to contribute to validating innovations in your area of expertise — to dedicate a few hours per month to evaluating whether an idea submitted by a stranger is physically coherent and original? Not to be paid, but because you yourself would benefit from the same service the day you had something to protect? This is cooperative logic in its simplest form. Is it sufficient to build something that lasts?

The next act of this book will name the shadow zones that this act has deliberately illuminated from their best angle: the real risks of sovereign communications, the limits of distributed trust against bad-faith actors, and the question that every architecture of freedom must confront — how to remain free without becoming a refuge for those whose freedom threatens that of others. These questions do not invalidate what this act has built. They complete the picture honestly.
